Cloaked in dense beech forest, it is regarded as a truly secluded peak, climbable only in the snow season via the Okususohana Nature Garden, with views of the Togakushi range and Northern Alps from its summit.

103047.description_ja_short: 群馬・新潟県境の三国峠に立つ標高1636mの山。木道が整備され展望もよい人気の山。

Naeba, drawing hikers with fresh greenery in spring and colorful foliage in autumn.
103047.description_en_short: A 1,636 m peak at Mikuni Pass on the Gunma-Niigata border, with a boardwalk trail and fine mountain views.

103050.description_en_long: Takenokoyama is a 1,790 m peak in Yuzawa, Niigata Prefecture, forming the summit area of the Naeba Ski Resort, which was developed by Seibu Group's Yoshiaki Tsutsumi and opened in 1961. In summer the peak can be reached by gondola, and it is popular for gathering nemagaridake (dwarf bamboo shoots).

103108.description_en_long: Mt. Taiheizan rises about 1,960 m along the ridge on the south shore of Lake Chuzenji, in the Ashio district of Nikko City, Tochigi Prefecture. It was formerly known as Mt. Matsuki after the old Matsuki village, though today's topographic maps label it Taiheizan.

Formed of quartz porphyry and rhyolite, it lies just off the historic Zencho Gyoja mountain-ascetic route, and its slopes turn pink with wild azaleas each spring.

Bishamonsan (587 m) in Nikko City, Tochigi Prefecture, is a popular hiking peak accessible from Otanimukai Station on the Tobu Kinugawa Line, developed as the Chausuyama hiking course. 103133.description_en_long: Mt. Toramiyama (446 m) sits on the border of Utsunomiya and Nikko cities in Tochigi Prefecture. It draws extra hikers in years of the Tiger in the Chinese zodiac, though the origin of its name is uncertain; one legend links it to castle construction spanning the Tiger and Snake years. The trailhead is at Yamaguchi in Nikko, with an easy round trip of about 2.5 km.
103133.description_en_short: A 446 m mountain on the Utsunomiya-Nikko border, popular with hikers during Tiger zodiac years.

Its summit holds a triangulation point, but there is no established trail; climbers typically use a loop route starting from Namezawa Onsen that also takes in neighboring Mt. Itasawa.

Sekisonsan (481 m) in Shioya Town, Tochigi Prefecture, takes its name from a belief that a mountain deity descended onto its summit rocks, making it an object of Sekison worship tied to rain-praying and agriculture. A shrine sits at the top with a triangulation point just behind it, and the mountain is usually climbed on a loop course from behind Shioya Town Sports Park.

Fuji from the summit.

Hiuchigatake, and distant Mt. Akagi.

103214.description_ja_long: 栃木県の奥日光に位置する標高2181mの山。夏場は濃密な笹薮に覆われ登山道がなく、湯元温泉から刈込湖を経て山頂を目指す。山頂周辺は樹林に囲まれ展望に乏しいが、少し南に下ると開けた草地から男体山などの日光連山を一望できる。

103214.description_en_long: Takanagiyama is a 2,181 m peak in the Oku-Nikko area of Tochigi Prefecture, covered in dense bamboo grass and lacking a marked trail. Climbers typically approach from Yumoto Onsen via Lake Karikomiko. The wooded summit offers little view, but an open meadow slightly south reveals Mt. Nantai and the Nikko mountain range.

Climbing records for this peak are scarce, and no confirmed information about its origin has been found; it remains a quiet, little-documented mountain.

103239.description_ja_short: 栃木県那須塩原市に位置する標高311mの山で、山頂に三島神社奥宮元芳神社が鎮座する丘。
103239.description_en_long: Akadayama is a 311 m hill in Nasushiobara City, Tochigi Prefecture. It served as a base for Chokosha, the first large-scale farm reclamation on the Nasuno Plain, launched in 1880 by Michitsune Mishima, who is said to have climbed the hill to plan the project. Its summit holds Motoyoshi Shrine, an inner shrine of Mishima Shrine, along with a viewing plaza.
103239.description_en_short: A 311 m hill in Nasushiobara, Tochigi, tied to the 1880 Chokosha farm reclamation project.

A small shrine called Sengen-sama sits at its summit, and a lion dance is offered there each September 1st to pray for good harvests and family safety.
